Category: Agriculture & Resources: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing, Mining, Energy
Description: Security and accuracy concerns in remote sensing and aerial monitoring.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how agricultural or resource industries operate, along with a general interest in data collection technologies such as drones or satellites, is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key security and accuracy risks associated with satellite and drone data collection across agriculture, forestry, fishing, mining, and energy sectors. You will also be able to explain how these risks arise in remote sensing and aerial monitoring operations, and apply practical strategies to safeguard data integrity in your organisation.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces the key risks associated with collecting, storing, and using data gathered from satellites and drones. You will explore how remote sensing technologies can raise serious concerns around data security, privacy, and accuracy, particularly when that data is used to inform important decisions. We will look at real-world examples of how satellite and drone data can be intercepted, manipulated, or misinterpreted, and what that means for organisations relying on it. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the practical steps that can be taken to reduce these risks and handle aerial and satellite data more responsibly. No prior technical knowledge is required, making this an ideal starting point for anyone working with or around remote sensing technologies.
lessons.content: Security and accuracy concerns in remote sensing and aerial monitoring.

slides.subtitle: Understanding the growing threat landscape
slides.slidecontent:
• Satellites and drones collect sensitive data on infrastructure, resources, and operations daily
• Agriculture, mining, energy, and forestry sectors increasingly depend on this technology
• Data breaches can expose critical business intelligence to competitors
• Security gaps in remote sensing systems create real operational risks
• Understanding these vulnerabilities is essential for responsible technology adoption

slides.subtitle: When sensor data leads you astray
slides.slidecontent:
• Sensor calibration drifts over time, reducing measurement accuracy
• Weather conditions distort readings and image quality
• GPS positioning errors compound during data collection
• Equipment malfunctions create systematic errors in datasets
• Poor data quality leads to flawed decision-making in critical operations

slides.subtitle: Redirecting aircraft through false signals
slides.slidecontent:
• Attackers broadcast fake GPS signals to deceive drone navigation systems
• Aircraft unknowingly follow false coordinates to unintended locations
• Cargo and valuable data can be intercepted during spoofed flights
• Remote attacks require no physical access to the aircraft
• Both military and civilian operations face this growing threat

slides.subtitle: Blocking navigation systems entirely
slides.slidecontent:
• Radio frequency interference overwhelms weak GPS signals
• Aircraft lose navigation capability and may enter emergency protocols
• Critical infrastructure sites experience frequent jamming incidents
• Border zones and conflict areas see the highest interference rates
• Operations in high-risk areas require alternative navigation backup systems

slides.subtitle: How navigation attacks compromise survey accuracy
slides.slidecontent:
• Corrupted positioning data invalidates entire survey missions
• Location timestamps become unreliable when GPS is compromised
• Survey boundaries shift unpredictably during spoofing attacks
• Geographic analysis fails when coordinate systems are manipulated
• Resource mapping and monitoring lose accuracy without reliable positioning

slides.subtitle: Why signal threats are becoming more common
slides.slidecontent:
• Jamming and spoofing equipment is increasingly affordable and available
• Technical knowledge barriers continue to decrease
• Attack range extends for miles from the interference source
• Multiple aircraft can be affected simultaneously
• Detection and attribution of attacks remains technically challenging

slides.subtitle: Real-world impacts across sectors
slides.slidecontent:
• Agricultural surveys produce inaccurate crop yield predictions
• Mining operations lose precision in resource identification
• Energy infrastructure monitoring fails to detect actual conditions
• Forestry management decisions based on corrupted boundary data
• Emergency response efforts misdirected by compromised location intelligence

slides.subtitle: Practical steps for data protection
slides.slidecontent:
• Verify data sources and validate positioning accuracy before use
• Encrypt sensitive imagery and maintain secure data transmission
• Confirm legal permissions and airspace clearances before operations
• Implement backup navigation systems for critical missions
• Establish incident response procedures for suspected data breaches
• Regularly audit and update security protocols as threats evolve

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on satellite and drone data risks. Remote sensing technology has become deeply embedded in how industries like agriculture, mining, energy, and forestry operate. Every day, satellites and drones collect detailed information about infrastructure, natural resources, and business operations. While this data drives important decisions, it also creates significant vulnerabilities. When competitors gain access to your survey data, they learn about your resource discoveries, operational patterns, and strategic plans. Security gaps in these systems aren't just technical problems—they create real business risks that can affect everything from competitive advantage to regulatory compliance. Understanding these vulnerabilities is the foundation for using remote sensing technology responsibly and safely.

Even when security isn't compromised, remote sensing data can still lead you astray due to technical limitations. Sensors naturally drift from their original calibration over time, which means measurements that were accurate last month might be systematically off today. Weather adds another layer of uncertainty—clouds, atmospheric moisture, and temperature variations all distort both imagery and sensor readings. GPS systems, which we often take for granted, can introduce positioning errors that compound throughout your data collection process. Equipment failures happen too, creating systematic errors that affect entire datasets. The real problem comes when these accuracy issues go undetected, because decisions made on flawed data can have serious operational consequences.

One of the most sophisticated threats facing drone operations is GPS spoofing, where attackers deliberately broadcast false navigation signals. Unlike jamming, which is obvious, spoofing is insidious because the aircraft has no idea anything is wrong. It receives what appear to be legitimate GPS coordinates and follows them faithfully, potentially flying directly into the hands of whoever is conducting the attack. This isn't theoretical—cargo drones have been successfully intercepted this way, and the sensitive data they carry compromised. What makes this particularly concerning is that the attack can be conducted remotely, with the perpetrator potentially miles away from the aircraft. Both military and civilian operations face this growing threat.

While spoofing tricks navigation systems, jamming takes a more direct approach by simply blocking GPS signals altogether. Radio frequency interference can overwhelm the relatively weak satellite signals that aircraft depend on, leaving them unable to determine their position. When this happens, most systems enter some kind of emergency protocol—perhaps returning to their launch point or hovering in place until the signal returns. You see this type of interference most frequently around critical infrastructure, where it may be used as a defensive measure, and in border regions or conflict zones where it serves tactical purposes. For operations planning to work in these high-risk areas, having backup navigation systems becomes essential.

The consequences of navigation attacks extend far beyond flight safety—they fundamentally compromise the integrity of data collection missions. When GPS coordinates are corrupted during a survey, every single data point collected becomes questionable. Location timestamps lose their reliability, survey boundaries shift unpredictably, and the geographic analysis that depends on accurate positioning starts to fail. For resource industries, this is particularly problematic because mapping and monitoring activities depend entirely on knowing exactly where measurements were taken. A corrupted agricultural survey might suggest crops are growing in the wrong fields, or a mining survey might place mineral deposits in locations that don't actually contain them.

What makes signal-based attacks particularly concerning is how accessible the technology has become. The equipment needed to jam or spoof GPS signals is no longer expensive or difficult to obtain, and the technical knowledge required to use it effectively continues to decrease. These attacks can be conducted from considerable distances—potentially affecting multiple aircraft simultaneously from a single interference source. This combination of accessibility, range, and impact makes detection and response challenging. Law enforcement and security services often struggle to identify who is conducting these attacks and where they're operating from, which means the threat landscape continues to evolve faster than defensive measures.

The real-world impact of these data risks varies significantly across different industries, but the consequences are consistently serious. In agriculture, corrupted survey data can lead to inaccurate crop yield predictions, affecting both harvesting decisions and market planning. Mining operations that rely on precise positioning might invest heavily in areas that don't actually contain the resources their surveys suggested. Energy companies monitoring infrastructure through aerial surveys could miss critical maintenance issues if their data is compromised. Forestry management decisions based on corrupted boundary data can lead to legal disputes and environmental damage. Even emergency response efforts can be misdirected when location intelligence systems provide unreliable information.

Protecting against these risks requires a multi-layered approach that addresses both technical vulnerabilities and operational procedures. Always verify your data sources and validate positioning accuracy before making important decisions based on remote sensing information. Sensitive imagery should be encrypted during transmission and storage to prevent unauthorized access. Before any flight operations, confirm you have proper legal permissions and airspace clearances in place. For missions in high-risk areas, implement backup navigation systems that don't rely solely on GPS. Establish clear incident response procedures so your team knows how to react if they suspect a data breach or navigation attack. Finally, treat security as an ongoing process—regularly audit your protocols and update them as new threats emerge.

Who is this for: Agricultural managers, farm operations staff, forestry supervisors, mining engineers, energy facility operators, GIS analysts, environmental compliance officers, data analysts in natural resources, operations managers in agriculture and energy sectors
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Environmental Compliance, Data Management, Agricultural Operations, Mining Operations, Energy Operations, GIS and Mapping, Risk Management
